GateDocument.TextWithNodes: "Iconic watches 101: the timepiece every classic dresser knows about", "Ever considered an investment watch? Meet the iconic timepiece that just got an update.", "In the rarefied world of watches, you would be forgiven for thinking you need to reach aficionado status before investing in a timepiece. But strip away the technicalities and intricate design feats, and all you need to know first, in any aesthete's book, is the power that an overall impression of a singular, thoughtfully-conceived and impeccably-executed piece can have. Enter TAG Heuer's Carrera Chronograph. Widely regarded as a timepiece that has earned its place in the classic stable of watches, the watch has re-emerged in a new collection created to mark the 160th anniversary of the Swiss watchmaker. Four new elegant contemporary reworks are on offer with with 42mm faces, revised proportions and come in a choice of two different, unisex and universally appealing straps: stainless steel bracelet and brown alligator. With racing roots, the watch's pedigree goes far in explaining its enduring appeal. Debuted in 1963, the TAG Heuer Carrera Chronograph was so-named after its creator, Jack Heuer, great grandson of the Swiss watchmaker's founder Edouard Heuer, an avid follower of motor sports was taken by the title of a car race through Mexico, the Carrera Panamericana. Heuer created the watch in a time when TAG Heuer was well-known for making timekeeping equipment and stopwatches for the professional racing circuit. With this in mind he put functionality and usability at its core, focusing on its legibility – note the face and its three subdials' minimalist sensibilities, free from any superfluous frills. The result was a pared back approach that has influenced watchmakers ever since. Horological commentators agree that the piece set the pace for wristwatches since its debut, with its no-fuss, streamlined approach to design winning favour with collectors and classic-dressers alike. Heuer, an admirer of industrial design and design at large (he followed closely the work of Eames and Oscar Niemeyer among others), made this his most beloved collection and it now holds a prized position in TAG Heuer's stable of pieces. After five decades, its 1960s appeal has held its allure, its design capturing the optimism and forward-thinking attitude of the era's space-age futurism, and original thinking where creativity thrived. The chronograph – being an in-built stopwatch – is also a direct link to its racing past. What is new though? Thinner bracelets created for ergonomic comfort on the wrist, link angles are rounded to dial-down any bulkiness while added design elements that appeared on subsequent editions have been stripped out, like the tachymeter scale, leaving the recognisable stainless-steel bezel as a focus. With a choice of black and blue dials on the bracelet renditions, and silver with rose gold-plated hands, or sleek anthracite with the alligator straps, there is an emphasis on colour palettes with versatility, to integrate into any wardrobe. And those technical bells and whistles? The Calibre Heuer 02 movement is a supercharged upgrade from the original – an in-house 168-component movement made at the Chevenez manufacture, all visible through the caseback. And as life picks back up again (is it just us, or is the pre-Christmas pace picking up a little too fast?) the TAG Heuer Carrera Chronograph is, like the original, is made to read at speed; an original goal of its progenitors was 'timing at a glance' – though we dare you to glance at its classic face without stopping for a longer admiring look. But if you're thinking of investing, you don't need to know all that. All you need to remember is that this is a piece that will continue transcending the decades, and leave a lingering impression only a bonafide icon, with 160-years of history at its foundation, can leave."
